text: The surgeon mobilizes the mesocolon by dissecting along the taut fascia. The duodenal mesenteric fossa is explored, and the mesenteric vein is identified at the lower border of the pancreas. Dissection continues inside the visceral peritoneum, medial to the inferior mesenteric vein. The mesentery is tented to create a retroperitoneal tunnel along the retroperitoneal fascia plane. The inferior mesenteric vein is clipped and transected, and the widest gyrota fascia is swept down to separate it from the overlying mesocolon.

The surgeon continues dissection along the taut fascia plane, progressing from medial to lateral direction. The dissection advances over the surface of the pancreas tail, entering the lesser sac. The peritoneum is incised over the bifurcation area of the abdominal aorta. With tenting of the mesocolon containing the superior rectal artery, a retroperitoneal tunnel is created from medial to lateral.

The surgeon continues to develop the dissection plane downward, transecting the vascular pedicle below the branching of the left colic artery. Continuous tenting and traction of the mesenteric vascular pedicle allows the fascia dissection to proceed downward. The ureter and gonadal vessel are visible and well preserved. At the sacral promontory, the taut fascia is observed fusing with the proper fascia of the rectum and endopelvic fascia.

The surgeon enters the presacral space after incising the fused fascia. The dissection shifts to the left lateral peritoneal reflection, where the lateral colonic attachment is separated along the white line of Toldt. The peritoneal reflection is tented, revealing the tapering and spot-like appearance of the Toldt fascia.

The surgeon continues the incision of peritoneal attachments along the white line of Toldt, extending upward to the lower border of the spleen and the tail of the pancreas. The left side colon is completely mobilized along the taut fascia.
